ops.title: jj rebase -r extracts only that revision; descendants (incl. @) are reparented onto the OLD base — follow with jj rebase -r @ -d <new tip> or use -s
ops.body:
`jj rebase -r <change> -d main@origin` (the AGENTS.md-recommended land-on-updated-remote flow) moves ONLY the named revision; its descendants — including the working-copy commit `@` — are reparented onto the revision's *former* parent, silently leaving `@` behind on the stale base.

**Why:** `-r` means "extract this one revision"; jj preserves descendants by stitching them to the old parent. Observed 2026-07-03 in cc-notes: after rebasing the settings commit onto the fetched main, `@` sat on the pre-fetch tip (80d69f83) instead of the new commit.

**How to apply:** after a `-r` rebase, check `jj log -r '@-'`; if `@` was a child of the moved revision, follow with `jj rebase -r @ -d <moved revision>`. When the working copy should ride along from the start, rebase the whole stack with `-s <change>` instead of `-r`. Related: [[jj-colocated-use-jj-not-git-stash]], [[jj-commit-snapshots-whole-worktree]].
